claim: The studied low-density steel has a composition of Fe30Mn6.5Al0.3C (wt.%), an austenitic (fcc) matrix free of ordered κ-carbides, and a weight reduction of ~8.5%.
evidence_text: We selected this steel composition as an example of austenitic FeMnAlC steel free of kappa-carbides with a high weight reduction (~8.5% density reduction) [1].

claim: The hydrogen-charged sample contains a total hydrogen concentration of 101 mass ppm, as determined by thermal desorption spectroscopy (Figure 1).
evidence_text: the total measured hydrogen concentration was 101 mass ppm.

claim: Hydrogen charging to 101 mass ppm results in a yield stress increase of 10% and an increase in elongation to fracture of ~8% under room temperature tensile deformation.
evidence_text: yield stress increase of 10%) and ductility (increase in the elongation to fracture of ~8%)

claim: Hydrogen promotes inhomogeneous plasticity via hydrogen-enhanced deformation banding (HEDB) and strain localization via hydrogen-enhanced microbanding (HEMB).
evidence_text: hydrogen promotes deformation mechanisms associated with inhomogeneous plasticity (hydrogen-enhanced deformation banding (HEDB)) and strain localization (hydrogenenhanced microbanding (HEMB)).

claim: The annealed Fe30Mn6.5Al0.3C (wt.%) low-density steel has a fully austenitic structure with an average grain size of 175 μm, which remained stable at room temperature.
evidence_text: The low-density steel investigated in the present study had the chemical composition Fe30Mn6.5Al0.3C (wt. %). ... The annealed material contains a fully austenitic structure with an average grain size of 175 μm, which remained stable at room temperature.

claim: ECCI was conducted at 15-20 kV using a solid-state four-quadrant BSE detector at 6–7 mm working distance in high-current mode, with samples final-polished with 250 nm colloidal silica suspension after sequential mechanical polishing.
evidence_text: ECCI was conducted at 15 - 20 kV using a solid-state four-quadrant backscatter electron (BSE) detector at a 6 – 7 mm working distance. ... ECCI samples were mechanically polished with 800 and 1200 SiC grit papers and subsequently polished with 9 and 3 μm diamond suspensions. Final polishing was performed with 250 nm colloidal silica suspension (Struers, OP-S).

claim: STEM was performed in a JEOL JEM 2800 TEM at 200 kV, and samples were prepared by in-situ FIB lift-out with final thinning at 5 kV and 200 pA to avoid surface damage.
evidence_text: STEM was performed in a JEOL JEM 2800 TEM operating at 200 kV. STEM samples were fabricated in areas of interest by the in-situ FIB lift-out method in a Scios 2 ThermoFisher Scientific dual-beam SEM system. The thinning of the FIB lamella was performed at an initial acceleration voltage of 30kV and a milling current of \~1000 pA on both sides of the sample followed by a final thinning procedure at 5 kV and 200 pA to avoid surface damage induced by FIB milling [39].

claim: After tensile testing, solute hydrogen concentration was measured by gas chromatography TDA at a heating rate of 100 K/h; the hydrogen-charged specimen showed one peak corresponding to lattice diffusion and contained 101 mass ppm H, while the uncharged sample contained less than 5 mass ppm H.
evidence_text: After the tensile testing, solute hydrogen concentration was measured by gas chromatography thermal desorption analysis (TDA) with a thermal conductivity detector at a heating rate of 100 K/h. ... The measured hydrogen concentration was 101 mass ppm. ... The estimated hydrogen concentration in the uncharged sample is < 5 mass ppm.

claim: In the uncharged sample, grain orientations were classified into Type 1a and Type 1b dislocation structures, with Type 1a containing geometrically necessary boundaries (GNB) and incidental dislocation boundaries (IDB), as shown in inverse pole figures (Figure 3a) and ECC images (Figure 4).
evidence_text: Figure 3. Inverse pole figures along the tensile axis direction of the grain orientations investigated in the uncharged sample (a) and hydrogen-charged sample (b). ... Figure 4. Classification of the dislocation structure in the uncharged sample. (a, b): Type 1a dislocation structure; (c, d): Type 1b dislocation structure. ... GNB: geometrically necessary boundary; IDB: incidental dislocation boundary.

claim: Solute hydrogen increases the yield stress by approximately 27 MPa and the flow stress by 14–25 MPa, with a higher hardening effect on YS than on FS.
evidence_text: the increase on YS is ~ 27 MPa, and that on FS is between 14 MPa (ε = 0.4) and 25 MPa (ε = 0.1).

claim: Both uncharged and hydrogen-charged samples exhibit similar strain-hardening behavior with four stages (A–D); stage B shows a constant hardening rate, stage C reaches a plateau at ε=0.11, and no extra hardening stage from deformation twinning is observed.
evidence_text: Fig. 2(b) shows that both uncharged and hydrogen-charged samples exhibit similar strain-hardening behavior, with four hardening stages (A-D). ... Stage B reveals a constant hardening rate ... Stage C ... reaching a plateau at ε = 0.11 ... No extra hardening stage associated with deformation twinning is observed.

claim: Type 2 dislocation structure is formed by dislocation cells delimited by IDBs and occurs in grains oriented close to [001]//TA, where octahedral slip with eight slip systems of similar Schmid factors is active.
evidence_text: Type 2 dislocation structure is formed by dislocation cells (DCs) delimited by IDBs, Figs. 5(a, b). This dislocation structure is formed in grains oriented close to [0 0 1]//TA directions where the slip type is octahedral, i.e. eight slip systems with similar Schmid factors.

claim: Quantitative ECCI/EBSD analysis of GNB and IDB misorientations involved about 250 GNBs (30-50 per grain orientation) yielding ~1500 GNB misorientation measurements, and about 1000 IDBs (~200 boundaries per grain) with one measurement per IDB, as shown in Fig. 6.
evidence_text: About 250 GNBs (30 - 50 boundaries per grain orientation) were analyzed in the uncharged and hydrogen-charged samples. 5 – 10 measurements per GNB were performed, resulting in a dataset of about 1500 GNB misorientations. About 1000 IDBs were analyzed (~200 boundaries per grain orientation). One measurement per IDB was performed in a dataset of about 1000 misorientations.

claim: Hydrogen increases the average IDB misorientation by approximately 0.25° compared to the uncharged condition.
evidence_text: the effect on θ̄_IDB^H is comparatively smaller than that on θ̄_GNB^H, as θ̄_IDB^H − θ̄_IDB ∼ 0.25°

claim: Hydrogen increases the average GNB misorientation (θ̄_GNB) by approximately 1° relative to the uncharged state.
evidence_text: and θ̄_GNB^H − θ̄_GNB ∼ 1°.

claim: Hydrogen strongly modifies the GNB misorientation distribution by increasing the population of θ_GNB^H > 2° and extending the distribution to values of ~7-8°.
evidence_text: it strongly modifies the θ_GNB^H distributions by increasing the population of misorientation values with θ_GNB^H > 2° and extending the θ_GNB^H distribution to larger values (∼ 7 − 8°).

claim: In Type 1a cell blocks, GNBs are composed of dense dislocation walls (DDWs) with an average thickness of 90 ± 10 nm, aligned parallel to the trace of the primary slip system (1 -1 1).
evidence_text: GNBs are formed by DDWs with an average thickness t_DDW = 90 ± 10 nm aligned parallel to the plane trace of the primary slip system (1 -1 1).

claim: Microbands (MBs) nucleating along GNB sections in Type 1a CBs are lenticular-shaped with thickness 150-200 nm and length 1.5-2.0 μm.
evidence_text: MBs are imaged as thick lenticular-shaped structures with a thickness t_MB = 150 - 200 nm and a length l_MB = 1.5 - 2.0 μm.

claim: In Type 1a CB, KAM-EBSD misorientation measurements yield θ_DDW1a^H = 1.0 ± 0.1°, θ_MB1a^H = 1.9 ± 0.1°, and θ_MB1b^H = 2.0 ± 0.2°.
evidence_text: Analysis of the boundary misorientations on the corresponding KAM-EBSD map (Fig. 8(c)) yields θ_DDW1a^H = 1.0 ± 0.1°, θ_MB1a^H = 1.9 ± 0.1°, and θ_MB1b^H = 2.0 ± 0.2°.

claim: GNB misorientations below 2° correspond to dense dislocation walls (DDWs), whereas values above 2° are associated with microbands (MBs).
evidence_text: These results indicate that GNB misorientations (θ and θ_GNB^H) < 2° correspond to DDWs, and GNB misorientations (θ_GNB and θ_GNB^H) > 2° are associated with MBs.

claim: In Type 1b cell blocks, microbands formed along GNB1 sections have longer lengths of ~3-4 µm and higher misorientations: θ_MB1a^H = 5.0 ± 0.2° and θ_MB1b^H = 5.6 ± 0.2°.
evidence_text: In this case, MBs are comparatively longer than those formed in Type 1a (l_MB ∼ 3-4 μm). Analysis of the boundary misorientation by KAM-EBSD yields θ_MB1a^H = 5.0 ± 0.2° and θ_MB1b^H = 5.6° ± 0.2°

claim: The hydrogen-enhanced population of GNBs with misorientation >2° is associated with the localized nucleation of microbands along GNBs, which raises the average GNB misorientation.
evidence_text: The present analysis demonstrates that the hydrogen-enhanced population of GNBs with θ_GNB^H > 2° is associated with the localized nucleation of MBs along GNBs, resulting in an increased θ̄_GNB^H.

claim: The diffraction pattern along the [101] zone axis shows no extra diffraction spots along <200> and <220> directions, confirming that ordered κ-carbides are absent in the Fe30Mn6.5Al0.3C steel.
evidence_text: Fig. 9(b) shows the corresponding diffraction pattern along the [1 0 1] zone axis. The diffraction pattern did not reveal the formation of extra diffraction spots along <200> and <220> directions, which indicates that ordered kappa-carbides were not formed in the present Fe30Mn6.5Al0.3C low-density steel

claim: Inside deformation band DB1 oriented close to [2 1 15]//TA, the hydrogen-charged sample contains equiaxed dislocation cells and one crystallographic microband system, with MB thickness 150–200 nm and spacing ~2–3 μm.
evidence_text: ECCI analysis of the dislocation structure of the DB interior consists of a combination of equiaxed DCs and one crystallographic MB system corresponding to the highly stressed (1 -1 1) and (-1 1 1) slip planes. Under the current channeling conditions, MBs are imaged as bright plates with an average thickness of 150 - 200 nm that are spaced ~ 2 - 3 μm.

claim: Hydrogen charging reduces the average dislocation cell size in Type 2 structures from 220 ± 10 nm (uncharged) to 192 ± 10 nm (hydrogen-charged), as measured from ECC images.
evidence_text: The analysis reveals that hydrogen refines the average DC size from ̅D_DC = 220 ± 10 nm (uncharged) to ̅D_DC^H = 192 ± 10 nm (hydrogen-charged).

claim: In a grain near [5 4 6]//TA of the hydrogen-charged sample, a well-developed DDW structure aligned with the primary (1 -1 1)[0 1 1] slip system contains dislocation cells of average size 210 ± 10 nm.
evidence_text: The dislocation structure is formed by a well-developed DDW structure that is aligned close to the primary slip system (1 -1 1) [0 1 1] (Schmid factor = 0.37). Within the DDW structure, DCs with an average size ̅D_DC^H = 210 ± 10 nm are formed.

claim: In the Type 3 microstructure of the hydrogen-charged sample, deformation bands of thickness ~0.5–1.0 μm and in-grain microbands with thickness ~200 nm and spacing ~2–3 μm are aligned with (-1 1 1) plane traces, indicating activation of secondary slip systems.
evidence_text: ECCI images reveal the propagation of DBs with thicknesses ~ 0.5 – 1.0 μm and closely spaced arrays (~ 2 – 3 μm) of in-grain MB plates through localized grain regions. The average thickness of the MB plates is similar to that of the DC structure, about 200 nm. Plane trace analysis by combined EBSD and ECCI confirms that DBs and MBs are aligned close to the (-1 1 1) plane traces.

claim: BF-STEM reveals that deformation bands in the hydrogen-charged sample produce larger shear offsets (~75–100 nm) on DDW segments compared to the uncharged sample; DBs are coarser (thickness ~0.5–1.0 μm vs ~0.3–0.5 μm) and tend to merge into coarse structures.
evidence_text: the magnitude of the shear offset produced by the DB-DDW intersections in the hydrogen-charged sample is comparatively larger. For instance, ... DB3 in the hydrogen-charged sample produces shear offsets of ~ 75 – 100 nm ... DBs in these grains ... are coarser in the hydrogen-charged sample (thickness ~ 0.5 – 1.0 μm) than those formed in the uncharged sample (thickness ~ 0.3 – 0.5 μm). As shown in Fig. 13(b), individual DBs tend to merge into coarse structures in the hydrogen-charged sample.

claim: STEM analysis inside a DB of the hydrogen-charged sample reveals a high density of long screw and mixed dislocations of approximately 1.3 × 10^14 m^−2.
evidence_text: STEM analysis indicates that the dislocation structure consists of a high density of long screw and mixed dislocations (~ 1.3 × 10^14 m^−2).

claim: In the hydrogen-charged sample, dissolved DDW segments are observed within DBs, attributed to the trapping of screw dislocations by DDWs, which makes these boundaries mechanically unstable.
evidence_text: dissolved DDW segments are visible (‘D’ in Figs. 15(a, b)). This effect is associated with the trapping of screw dislocations by DDWs, which makes them mechanically unstable.

claim: In the hydrogen-charged sample, interaction of primary (1 -1 1)[0 1 1] and secondary (1 -1 -1)[-1 0 -1] dislocations promotes the formation of sessile Lomer-Cottrell dislocations with Burgers vector [-1 1 0].
evidence_text: the interaction between (1 -1 1) [0 1 1] primary dislocations and (1 -1 -1) [-1 0 -1] secondary dislocations promotes the formation of [-1 1 0] sessile Lomer-Cottrell dislocations.

claim: Screw and mixed dislocations in DBs exhibit numerous jogs that act as pinning points for single-ended dislocation sources; cross-slip and double cross-slip are activated, exemplified by a (-1 1 1)[1 0 1] screw dislocation cross-slipping onto the (-1 -1 1) plane and back.
evidence_text: Screw and mixed dislocations exhibit numerous small cusps associated with jogs (‘J’ in Fig. 15(c)). These cusps play a key role in the dislocation multiplication process as they act as pinning points for single-ended sources of dislocations. Analysis of the alignment of the dislocation lines reveals cross-slipping and double-cross slipping dislocations (‘CS’ in Figs. 15(c, d)). The (-1 1 1) [1 0 1] screw dislocation cross-slips onto the (-1 -1 1) plane and then cross-slips back onto another (-1 1 1) plane.

claim: For Type 3 dislocation structures, hydrogen refines the average dislocation cell size from 236 ± 10 nm to 212 ± 10 nm, while the mean cell boundary misorientation remains almost unchanged (0.87 ± 0.05° uncharged vs 0.90 ± 0.05° hydrogen-charged).
evidence_text: hydrogen refines the average DC size from ̅D_DC = 236 ± 10 nm (uncharged) to ̅D_DC^H = 212 ± 10 nm (hydrogen-charged). ... EBSD data indicates that hydrogen plays a small effect on ̅θ_DC^H, as both misorientation populations are similar with ̅θ_DC = 0.87° ± 0.05° (uncharged sample) and ̅θ_DC^H = 0.90° ± 0.05° (hydrogen-charged sample).

claim: The solute hydrogen-induced strengthening in the present steel corresponds to a yield stress increase of 27 MPa, which is smaller than the ~60 MPa increase observed in 310S stainless steel under similar conditions.
evidence_text: The strengthening associated with solute hydrogen observed in the present steel (27 MPa) is smaller than that typically observed in austenitic stainless steels. For instance, in our recent study [64], we obtained a hydrogen-induced increase of the yield stress of ~60 MPa in a 310S stainless steel hydrogen-charged and tested at similar conditions to those used in this study.

claim: The dislocation cell size refinement in hydrogen-charged fcc metals has been ascribed to dislocation trapping by incidental boundaries due to Cottrell atmospheres of hydrogen, but the small refinement (~10%) in the present steel suggests this trapping effect is limited.
evidence_text: The dislocation cell size refinement reported in hydrogen-charged fcc metals has been ascribed to dislocation trapping by incidental boundaries due to Cottrell atmospheres of hydrogen [19]. ... The small influence of solute hydrogen in the DC structure of the present steel suggests that dislocation trapping by incidental boundaries induced by hydrogen is limited in the present steel.

claim: At 295 K and initial strain rate of 5.0×10⁻⁴ s⁻¹, hydrogen has a small influence on dislocation cells (cell size refinement ~10%) while enhancing the formation of dense dislocation walls.
evidence_text: The present study shows that at the current deformation conditions (295 K and initial strain rate of $5 . 0 \times$ $1 0 ^ { - 4 } s ^ { - 1 }$ ), hydrogen has a small influence on DCs (cell size refinement of $\sim 1 0 \% )$ while enhancing the formation of DDWs.

claim: Hydrogen has a greater influence on geometrically necessary boundaries (GNBs) than on incidental dislocation boundaries (IDBs), consistent with prior studies in fcc metals.
evidence_text: These findings suggest that hydrogen has a greater influence on GNBs than on IDBs, which agrees with former studies in fcc metals $[ 1 9 , 2 1 , 2 3 ]$.

claim: BF-STEM (Fig. 15) reveals high plastic activity in submicron DBs of the hydrogen-charged sample near <111>//TA, with secondary dislocation density ~1–3×10¹⁴ m⁻² causing plasticity localization.
evidence_text: BF-STEM images (Fig. 15) reveal a high plastic activity in the interior of submicron DBs formed in the grain oriented close to <1 1 1>//TA directions of the hydrogen-charged sample. The intense plastic activity is associated with the glide of secondary dislocations (dislocation density $\bf \tau \sim 1 - 3 \times 1 0 ^ { 1 4 } m ^ { - 2 } )$, resulting in the localization of plasticity.

claim: The critical stress for cross-slip of a screw segment of length 50–75 nm is calculated to be ~350–520 MPa, exceeding the estimated applied resolved shear stress of ~330 MPa, indicating that internal stress fields of DBs promote dislocation cross-slip.
evidence_text: If we calculate the stress required to expand a screw segment of length L onto the cross-slip plane, τcritical = 1.5 Gb/L [54], ... it yields $\tau _ { c r i t i c a l } \sim 3 5 0$ - 520 MPa for $L = 5 0 \mathrm { ~ - ~ } 7 5$ nm (estimated from BF-STEM images of cross-slipped dislocations). The calculated stress range is higher than the resolved shear stress estimated from the applied load, τ \~ 330 MPa. This analysis indicates that the internal stress field of DBs, mainly associated with the long-range stress field of the internal dislocation configuration, promotes dislocation cross-slip.

claim: Hydrogen (101 mass ppm) does not alter the ductile fracture mode but refines the dimple size, an effect attributed to void sheets generated by hydrogen-enhanced strain localization (Fig. 17).
evidence_text: The analysis of the fracture surfaces of the uncharged and hydrogencharged samples indicates that hydrogen does not modify the ductile fracture behavior of the steel. We find that hydrogen refines the dimple size. This effect is associated with void sheets produced by hydrogenenhanced strain localization phenomena.

claim: Hydrogen slightly shifts the type 3 cell boundary misorientation to higher angles (0.5° fraction increases from 0.08 to 0.13 and 1.5° from 0.05 to 0.11) and modifies the cell size distribution by increasing the fraction of ~200 nm cells from 0.16 to 0.30 while decreasing the 250 nm fraction from 0.24 to 0.18 (Fig. 16).
evidence_text: Figure 16. Effect of hydrogen on the distributions of the cell boundary misorientation, θ_DC, (a) and cell size, D_DC, (b) in type 3 dislocation structures. D_DC was estimated on ECC images. θ_DC was obtained from EBSD data.

claim: The Fe30Mn6.5Al0.3C steel containing 101 mass ppm hydrogen exhibits a negative IHE of -0.045 (increased ductility), a yield stress increase of ~10%, and an elongation to fracture increase of ~8% at 295 K and initial strain rate 5.0×10^-5 s^-1, with UTS around 580 MPa (Table 2, Fig. 19).
evidence_text: The present Fe30Mn6.5Al0.3C low-density steel exhibits a high HE resistance associated with a moderated increase in strength (yield stress increase of 10%) and ductility (increase in the elongation to fracture of ~ 8%). ... Table 2 ... 63.2Fe-30Mn-6.5Al-0.3C ... 104 ... -0.045 (HEMB) ... Fig. 19

claim: The dislocation structures that assist hydrogen-induced microband nucleation form at strain levels ε ~ 0.1–0.3, indicating that HEMB can be an active deformation mechanism in moderate-to-high ductility fcc alloys exposed to hydrogen.
evidence_text: The strain level required to form the dislocation structures that assist in the hydrogen-induced MB nucleation mechanisms (ε ~ 0.1 – 0.3) indicates that HEMB can be a relevant deformation mechanism in moderated-high ductile fcc alloys when exposed to hydrogen.

claim: Hydrogen has a minor effect on dislocation cells (cell size refinement of ~10%) but significantly enhances the formation of dense dislocation walls, indicating a stronger hydrogen stabilization effect on primary dislocation configurations with reduced dislocation spacing.
evidence_text: Hydrogen has a small influence on dislocation cells (cell size refinement of ~ 10%) while enhancing the formation of dense dislocation walls. These findings indicate that the influence of hydrogen on the stabilization of primary dislocation configurations with reduced dislocation spacing is comparatively stronger than dislocation trapping by incidental boundaries induced by hydrogen.
